patterns.title: Design Patterns
patterns.subtitle: 23 classic Gang of Four patterns
patterns.page_subtitle: All 23 classic Gang of Four patterns for object-oriented software design.
patterns.intro_p1: A design pattern is a general, battle-tested solution to a problem that recurs often in software design. It isn't ready-made code — it's a reusable template you adapt to your own situation.
patterns.intro_authors: All 23 patterns come from the book "Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software" (1994) — its authors, Erich Gamma, Richard Helm, Ralph Johnson, and John Vlissides, became known as the "Gang of Four" (GoF), and the book is still the standard reference for object-oriented design vocabulary.
patterns.intro_p2: The patterns fall into three groups: creational patterns handle object creation, structural patterns compose classes and objects into larger structures, and behavioral patterns manage responsibilities and communication between objects.
patterns.intro_p3: Knowing these patterns helps developers speak a shared vocabulary, read unfamiliar code faster, and reach for proven solutions instead of reinventing the wheel.

category_desc.creational: Deal with object creation mechanisms, aiming to create objects in a manner suitable to the situation.
category_desc.structural: Deal with object composition, creating relationships between objects to form larger structures.
category_desc.behavioral: Deal with communication between objects, defining how objects interact and distribute responsibility.

patterns.coming_soon_message: We are working on the full content for this pattern. Check back soon.
